updates.summary: Tract withdrew its $14B, 1,000-acre 'Project Range' datacenter proposal in western Maricopa County in May 2024 after the adjacent cities of Goodyear and Buckeye formally opposed the rezoning, citing incompatibility with surrounding residential neighborhoods. Building heights and noise were key concerns raised by city staff.

Both Goodyear and Buckeye city governments formally opposed Tract's $14B datacenter rezoning in the adjacent unincorporated Maricopa County area. City staff outlined concerns about building heights, noise, and emergency resources, leading to the project's withdrawal. Tract later proposed a new 1,700-acre complex in Buckeye near the airport with mayoral support.

Dallas-based Provident Realty Advisors withdrew its $1.3B datacenter proposal for the shuttered Brassie Golf Course in Chesterton after intense resident backlash. A petition attracted over 700 signatures. The Town Council stated the project could never be supported at its proposed scale and location. Concerns spanned air, water, energy, wetlands, wildlife, property values, and mental health impacts.

SoftBank subsidiary SB Energy is building and operating OpenAI's 1.2GW Stargate datacenter in Milam County. While no organized local opposition group has been identified, broader Texas concerns about datacenter water and energy consumption apply. SB Energy plans to build new generation capacity to support the facility and claims it will minimize water usage and protect Texas ratepayers.

The No Desert Data Center Coalition organized fierce opposition to Beale Infrastructure's 750MW Project Blue datacenter in Marana. Despite collecting over 2,800 petition signatures in just four days and filing a lawsuit alleging Open Meeting Act violations, the Marana Town Council unanimously approved the rezoning in January 2026. The coalition has expanded to oppose multiple Southern Arizona datacenter projects and has traveled nationally to share their organizing playbook.

The first Stargate AI datacenter site is in Abilene, with Crusoe securing $11.6B in financing. Residents near the Lancium project demanded transparency at Planning and Zoning Commission meetings, raising concerns about power, water, light/sound pollution, traffic, and safety. The project has 5,000+ construction workers and raised housing crisis concerns. Opposition was limited compared to other Texas sites, with individual residents like former school board member Samuel Garcia raising questions.

DC BLOX built a 120MW hyperscale facility (Atlanta West) in Lithia Springs in Douglas County, adjacent to Microsoft and Google facilities. DC BLOX reportedly experienced community opposition that derailed a different project in the area, and held at least two dozen meetings with county officials and community groups. Amazon also purchased 118 acres in Douglas County for a possible datacenter amid sustainability concerns.

Applied Digital is building a $7B AI datacenter campus on 900 acres in Ellendale (pop. ~1,000). Residents wearing 'No Eminent Domain' t-shirts packed city meetings to oppose the project, citing lack of representation. Concerns include power consumption, grid stability, and future land development impacts. An annexation battle has emerged. The company attempted community engagement by funding improvements to a local bowling alley.

A grassroots group gathered roughly 800 petition signatures opposing a proposed 1.5GW datacenter in Blakely Borough. The developer withdrew its zoning application in September 2025 after community pushback. The facility would have consumed 300,000 gallons of water per day and required as much power as the nearby Lackawanna Energy Center gas plant. Blakely subsequently enacted over 30 new restrictions on datacenters.

College Station residents mounted strong opposition to Priority Power's proposed 600MW datacenter on 200 acres of Midtown Drive. More than 75 individuals registered to speak in person and a petition with over 5,000 signatures was submitted. Concerns included noise pollution, procedural issues, environmental impacts, and utility demands. The city rejected the land sale and is now proactively considering guardrails for future datacenter proposals.

Twiggs County commissioners unanimously approved Eagle Rock Partners' $5B datacenter rezoning despite hundreds of residents packing the meeting in opposition. The crowd erupted in boos after the vote. Landowners filed a lawsuit alleging improper zoning notices (wrong property, incorrect hearing date) and failure to conduct a Development of Regional Impact study. Construction cannot begin until 2028 due to power infrastructure delays.

Prologis purchased 141 acres for its 'Project Mila' datacenter in Trenton. Residents Barry and Lorie Blankenship formed W.A.T.E.R. (Woodsdale and Trenton Environmental Resistance), which had 248 Facebook members. The group filed multiple public records requests seeking information about NDAs, environmental analyses, and health studies. Residents expressed concerns about higher energy/water bills, noise and light pollution, and feeling left in the dark.

Project Kestrel proposes six hyperscale datacenters totaling 1.8 million sq ft north of Kansas City International Airport with massive property and sales tax subsidies. Platte County Presiding Commissioner Scott Fricker publicly opposes the project, stating it will drive up water and utility rates for households and small businesses while giving subsidies to the world's richest corporations. Residents lack clarity on who is behind the project, who pays for power upgrades, and why there was no public input.

updates.company: Bristolville 25 Developer LLC / BHGH Properties LLC
updates.summary: Lordstown village council banned all datacenters in November 2025 in response to a $3.6B proposal from Bristolville 25 Developer LLC for the former GM complex site. Dozens of residents attended meetings voicing concerns about noise, lighting, water use, and utility rate increases. The developer sued and took the case to the Ohio Supreme Court. The village later replaced the ban with a temporary moratorium.

The $7B Stargate datacenter in Saline Township faces fierce opposition from residents and environmental groups. Over 5,000 public comments were submitted to the Michigan Public Service Commission, with the vast majority opposed. Residents call the project 'uniquely evil' for destroying wetlands, straining the grid, and reshaping their farming community. Legal challenges allege Open Meetings Act violations in the settlement that allowed the project to proceed. DTE's 1.4GW power contract was approved 3-0 despite opposition.

Denver-based Amp Z proposed a datacenter at the former Southland Paper Mill site near Lufkin, with 175MW of grid capacity rising to 1.1GW by 2028. A Change.org petition opposing the project received over 1,000 signatures. The Lufkin city council denied an Oncor substation permit after resident opposition. City officials emphasized the site's industrial history and zoning compatibility.

Tract acquired 1,060 acres near Silver Springs in Lyon County to develop a datacenter park with up to 1.6GW capacity. The Lyon County Planning Commission unanimously approved the rezoning of 500+ acres. Some citizens expressed opposition citing environmental impacts and accusations of official corruption. Microsoft is also developing a 274-acre datacenter in Silver Springs. The first phase provides 150MW across 2-4 buildings.

Amazon/AWS proposed a $4B datacenter on 471 acres in Wilmington with a 30-year, 100% property tax abatement (effective 70% abatement). Jessica Sharp organized the Facebook group 'Wilmington Residents for Responsible Development' which grew to nearly 3,000 members. WRRD demands Tier 4 EPA generators, air quality monitoring, closed-loop cooling, and a Community Benefits Agreement. Daily water demand could exceed 1 million gallons. Retired executive Kurt Cooper serves as liaison to officials.

opposition_groups: Kenoshans Unite Against Microsoft Data Center
updates.summary: Microsoft purchased 240 acres in Kenosha after signing an NDA with the city in May 2024. A Facebook page 'Kenoshans Unite Against Microsoft Data Center' run by Olivia Walker has mobilized opposition with videos and infographics. A petition against the project received over 20,000 signatures, and a viral TikTok got nearly 250,000 views. Residents protested a planned natural gas plant in nearby Paris township. At least 4 Wisconsin communities signed secrecy deals for datacenter projects.

St. Joseph County Council rejected a $13B datacenter rezoning 7-2 after a marathon 10-hour meeting, following an earlier unanimous unfavorable recommendation from the Area Plan Commission. Citizens for Responsible Economic Development led opposition focused on removal of prime farmland, water system impacts, and utility rate hikes. Amazon already has an $11B datacenter under construction nearby. A councilwoman claims her family was 'swatted' during the process.

Applied Digital proposed a $16B AI datacenter campus in the rural town of Toronto in Deuel County, South Dakota. Residents raised concerns about water use, electricity costs, housing capacity, and long-term community impacts. A critical blow came when the state Senate narrowly voted down SB 177 (17-18), a sales and use tax refund bill for datacenters, with Applied Digital stating the project is not viable without such incentives.

Meta is investing $1.5B in a 1GW datacenter on 1,000 acres in northeast El Paso. The Sembrando Esperanza Coalition, an economic and environmental justice group, called on the city to terminate contracts with Meta after learning El Paso Electric planned a 366MW natural gas plant to power the facility. Organizer Veronica Carbajal criticized the lack of transparency, noting they were told Meta would rely on renewable energy. Water usage could reach 1.5 billion gallons/year in a city already ranked among the worst for smog.

Eneus Energy's RGV Data Center Project near Harlingen would bring $14B in capital investment to Cameron County. Harlingen residents raised concerns about water and electricity use at public meetings. Cameron County Judge Eddie Trevino acknowledged limited county authority over the project. City and county officials told incensed residents they have little power to limit datacenters on private land.

Montour County commissioners unanimously denied Talen Energy's rezoning of 800+ acres from agricultural to industrial for a datacenter and power station. The Concerned Citizens of Montour County Facebook group amassed 2,300 followers after starting with just 4 members in August 2025. Their first town hall drew 120 people, and a petition gathered 3,000 signatures -- twice the township's population. The group retained a zoning/land use lawyer. Opposition was fueled by proximity to a nature preserve and residential area.

Atlanta-based Beltline Energy pitched a $16B, 16-building datacenter campus on 500 acres requiring annexation into Pacific. Hundreds of residents packed Pacific High School in opposition. The Missouri Department of Conservation flagged impacts on bald eagles, bats, eastern hellbender, fish, mussels, and plants. Residents fear well water depletion from estimated 1M gallons/day cooling needs. Beltline withdrew its rezoning request minutes into a special meeting, citing incomplete funding agreements. Community groups hired an environmental attorney.

Amazon/STACK Infrastructure plans a $12B multi-site datacenter network across Caddo and Bossier parishes. The Shreveport Metropolitan Planning Commission voted down one proposed site. A key concern is that the facility would use 7.5 million gallons of potable water per day, threatening Caddo Lake (an internationally protected wetland). The Sierra Club offered to pay all litigation fees. Mooringsport Mayor Tyler Gordon and Caddo Lake association members organized opposition. Bossier Parish has kept datacenter discussions largely off public agendas.

Developer Panattoni's 1GW 'Project Cannoli' datacenter would use 2.6-3 million gallons of water per day on 230 acres in Van Buren Township. A petition gathered 1,400 signatures opposing the project. Despite community pushback, the planning commission voted 5-2 to approve a preliminary plan in February 2026. Residents continue expressing concerns about noise, utility usage, and wildlife impacts as datacenter development expands across southeast Michigan.

Starwood Digital Ventures proposed 'Project Washington,' a 1.2GW datacenter on 580 acres near Delaware City. The facility would consume as much power as ~1 million homes, nearly twice all housing units in Delaware. Consumer advocates predict power bills could rise $70/month. Delaware's DNREC denied the Coastal Zone permit, ruling the project is 'heavy industry not compatible with the Coastal Zone.' Starwood is appealing. Dozens of residents pushed back at a tense town hall.

opposition_groups: Walla Walla chapter, Democratic Socialists of America
updates.summary: AWS acquired 554 acres in the Wallula Gap Business Park for $36M. Port of Walla Walla commissioners unanimously approved the sale despite vocal community opposition at the meeting. Critics including DSA members and College Place Mayor Norma Hernandez raised concerns about water consumption being 'ecologically devastating' in dry Eastern Washington, greenhouse emissions, and noise. The port framed it as a routine real estate transaction.
